la-axioms.title: Bracket axioms and matrix Lie algebras

questions.q: Which identity, beyond bilinearity and skew-symmetry $[x,y]=-[y,x]$, is the defining axiom of a Lie algebra?
questions.explain: The Jacobi identity is the third bracket axiom. ($[x,x]=0$ is equivalent to skew-symmetry in characteristic $\neq 2$ and is therefore not the extra defining condition.) Lie algebras are typically not associative — $\mathrm{ad}$ is a derivation, not a homomorphism.
questions.hint: It is the cyclic-sum identity that makes $\mathrm{ad}(x)$ a derivation of the bracket.

questions.explain: $\mathfrak{sl}_n$ is the traceless matrices: $\dim\mathfrak{sl}_n=n^2-1$. For $n=3$ that is $9-1=8$.
questions.hint: $n^2$ matrix entries minus one trace constraint.

questions.q: Which set of matrices forms a Lie subalgebra of $\mathfrak{gl}_n$ under the commutator $[X,Y]=XY-YX$?
questions.explain: If $X^\top=-X$ and $Y^\top=-Y$, then $[X,Y]^\top=(XY-YX)^\top=Y^\top X^\top - X^\top Y^\top = YX-XY=-[X,Y]$, so the skew-symmetric matrices $\mathfrak{so}_n$ are bracket-closed. Invertibility is not preserved (and $0$ is not invertible); symmetric matrices are not bracket-closed; trace-$1$ is not even a subspace.

questions.q: Why is $\mathrm{ad}\colon\mathfrak g\to\mathrm{End}(\mathfrak g)$ a Lie algebra homomorphism?
choices: Because the Killing form is bilinear
questions.explain: Apply both sides to $z$: $[\mathrm{ad}(x),\mathrm{ad}(y)]\,z=[x,[y,z]]-[y,[x,z]]$, while $\mathrm{ad}([x,y])\,z=[[x,y],z]$. These agree iff $[x,[y,z]]+[y,[z,x]]+[z,[x,y]]=0$, the Jacobi identity. So $\mathrm{ad}$ is a Lie homomorphism precisely because of Jacobi.
questions.hint: Expand $[\mathrm{ad}(x),\mathrm{ad}(y)]$ acting on a third element $z$.

questions.q: The Killing form $B(x,y)=\mathrm{tr}(\mathrm{ad}(x)\mathrm{ad}(y))$ is $\mathfrak g$-invariant in the sense:
choices: $B$ is positive-definite on every Lie algebra
questions.explain: Invariance means $B$ is annihilated by the adjoint action: differentiating $B(\mathrm{Ad}(g)x,\mathrm{Ad}(g)y)=B(x,y)$ gives $B([z,x],y)+B(x,[z,y])=0$. Equivalently, $\mathrm{ad}(z)$ is skew with respect to $B$. The Killing form is rarely positive-definite (it is on compact real forms only).

questions.q: For abelian $\mathfrak g$ (so $[x,y]=0$ identically), the Killing form is:
choices: The standard inner product on $\mathbb R^n$
questions.explain: If $\mathfrak g$ is abelian then $\mathrm{ad}(x)=0$ for every $x$, so $B(x,y)=\mathrm{tr}(0\cdot 0)=0$ identically. This is the cleanest example of a degenerate Killing form — abelian Lie algebras are the opposite of semisimple.
questions.hint: $\mathrm{ad}(x)=[x,-]$; what is it when the bracket vanishes?

la-solvable-and-nilpotent.title: Solvable, nilpotent, and the structural series

questions.q: The derived series of $\mathfrak g$ is $\mathfrak g^{(0)}=\mathfrak g$, $\mathfrak g^{(k+1)}=[\mathfrak g^{(k)},\mathfrak g^{(k)}]$. $\mathfrak g$ is solvable iff:
questions.explain: Solvability of a Lie algebra is the direct analogue of solvability of a group: the derived series eventually terminates at $0$. Nilpotence is the stronger condition that the lower-central series $\mathfrak g^0=\mathfrak g$, $\mathfrak g^{k+1}=[\mathfrak g,\mathfrak g^k]$ terminates; nilpotent implies solvable but not conversely.
questions.hint: Each step of the derived series replaces $\mathfrak h$ by $[\mathfrak h,\mathfrak h]$ — solvability says you eventually hit $0$.

questions.q: Engel's theorem says: $\mathfrak g$ is nilpotent iff
choices: $\mathrm{ad}(x)$ is a nilpotent endomorphism for every $x\in\mathfrak g$
Every element of $\mathfrak g$ is itself a nilpotent matrix
questions.explain: Engel: $\mathfrak g$ is nilpotent precisely when each $\mathrm{ad}(x)$ is a nilpotent endomorphism — pointwise nilpotence of the adjoint forces uniform nilpotence of the lower-central series. (Even though abstract elements need not be matrices, $\mathrm{ad}(x)$ always is.) Lie's theorem is the parallel statement for solvable: every solvable rep over $\mathbb C$ is upper-triangularizable.
questions.hint: It is a statement purely about the operators $\mathrm{ad}(x)$, not about the abstract elements.

questions.q: Which of the following Lie algebras is solvable but not nilpotent?
questions.explain: Upper-triangular $2\times 2$ matrices form the Borel $\mathfrak b_2$. Its derived algebra is the strictly upper-triangular line, which is abelian, so the derived series terminates — solvable. But $\mathrm{ad}(\mathrm{diag}(a,b))$ acting on $E_{12}$ has eigenvalue $a-b\neq 0$, so $\mathrm{ad}$ is not nilpotent. The Heisenberg algebra is nilpotent (so also solvable); diagonals are abelian; $\mathfrak{sl}_2$ is semisimple, not solvable.
questions.hint: Look at a Borel — derived once, then abelian, but $\mathrm{ad}$ has nonzero eigenvalues.

questions.q: Cartan's criterion characterizes semisimple Lie algebras as those with:
questions.explain: A finite-dimensional Lie algebra over a field of characteristic zero is semisimple iff its Killing form $B$ is nondegenerate. Equivalently, the radical (largest solvable ideal) is zero. Trivial center is necessary but not sufficient; semisimple Lie algebras do have only inner derivations, but that is a consequence, not the criterion.
questions.hint: It is the bilinear-form condition on $B(x,y)=\mathrm{tr}(\mathrm{ad}(x)\mathrm{ad}(y))$.

questions.q: A Cartan subalgebra $\mathfrak h\subset\mathfrak g$ of a complex semisimple Lie algebra is:
choices: A self-normalizing nilpotent subalgebra; equivalently for semisimple $\mathfrak g$, a maximal toral one
questions.explain: The general definition is: $\mathfrak h$ is nilpotent and equals its own normalizer. For complex semisimple $\mathfrak g$ this is equivalent to being a maximal toral (ad-diagonalizable) subalgebra — the diagonal matrices in $\mathfrak{sl}_n$. Borels are maximal solvable, strictly larger than $\mathfrak h$. Maximal abelian is too weak in general (though it coincides with Cartan in the semisimple case).
questions.hint: Two equivalent characterizations: nilpotent and self-normalizing, or maximal toral (in the semisimple case).

questions.q: The root system $A_2$ (associated with $\mathfrak{sl}_3$) has how many roots?
questions.explain: $\mathfrak{sl}_3$ has Cartan subalgebra of rank $2$ and dimension $8=2+6$. The six nonzero roots are $\pm(\alpha_1)$, $\pm(\alpha_2)$, $\pm(\alpha_1+\alpha_2)$ — the vertices of a regular hexagon in $\mathfrak h^*$.

questions.q: In the standard $\mathfrak{sl}_2$-triple $\{e,h,f\}$, the bracket relations are:
questions.explain: With the standard generators $e=\begin{pmatrix}0&1\\0&0\end{pmatrix}$, $h=\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&-1\end{pmatrix}$, $f=\begin{pmatrix}0&0\\1&0\end{pmatrix}$ one computes directly $[h,e]=2e$, $[h,f]=-2f$, $[e,f]=h$. The factor $2$ on the diagonal action is exactly the root pairing $\alpha(h)=2$.
questions.hint: Compute with the standard upper/lower triangular and diagonal $2\times 2$ matrices.

questions.q: Which sequence of weights occurs in the irreducible $\mathfrak{sl}_2$-representation $V_4$ of dimension $5$?
questions.explain: $V_n$ has highest weight $n$ and weights $n,n-2,\dots,-n$, each with multiplicity $1$. For $n=4$: $\{4,2,0,-2,-4\}$. The action of $f$ steps from $V_n$ down by $2$ at a time, so weights are integers of the same parity as $n$.
questions.hint: Highest weight is $n=4$; the $f$-orbit subtracts $2$ at each step.

questions.q: In the Clebsch–Gordan rule $V_m\otimes V_n=\bigoplus_{k=0}^{\min(m,n)}V_{m+n-2k}$, the decomposition of $V_2\otimes V_2$ is:
questions.explain: With $m=n=2$ and $k=0,1,2$: $V_{4}\oplus V_{2}\oplus V_{0}$. This matches the spin-$1\otimes$ spin-$1$ decomposition into spin-$2$, spin-$1$, spin-$0$ familiar from physics. Dimensions: $5+3+1=9=3\cdot 3$.
questions.hint: $k$ runs from $0$ to $\min(m,n)=2$; dimensions must add to $9$.

questions.q: Which classical series corresponds to the symplectic Lie algebra $\mathfrak{sp}_{2n}$?
questions.explain: The Cartan labels for the four classical infinite series are $A_n=\mathfrak{sl}_{n+1}$, $B_n=\mathfrak{so}_{2n+1}$, $C_n=\mathfrak{sp}_{2n}$, $D_n=\mathfrak{so}_{2n}$. So symplectic is $C_n$.
questions.hint: Mnemonic: A special-linear, B/D orthogonal (odd/even), C symplectic.

questions.q: How many exceptional simple complex Lie algebras are there?
questions.explain: The exceptions are $G_2$, $F_4$, $E_6$, $E_7$, $E_8$ — five total. Together with the four classical series $A,B,C,D$ they exhaust the simple complex Lie algebras (Killing–Cartan classification, late 19th century).

questions.q: A Dynkin diagram for a simple complex Lie algebra is connected. Which feature distinguishes the diagram of $G_2$ from those of the classical $A,B,C,D$ series?
choices: It has a trivalent branch node
It has a triple-bond edge between its two nodes
It has more than one connected component
questions.explain: $G_2$ is the unique simple Lie algebra of rank $2$ with a triple bond — the two simple roots have ratio $\sqrt 3$ in length and their angle is $150^\circ$. $A_2$ has a single bond, $B_2=C_2$ has a double bond. Trivalent branch nodes appear in $D_n,E_6,E_7,E_8$. A simple Lie algebra has connected Dynkin diagram by definition.
questions.hint: Among rank-$2$ root systems, the bond multiplicities are $1,2,3$ for $A_2,B_2,G_2$.
